title: Share of cancer deaths attributed to risk factors
subtitle: The estimated share of deaths from [cancers](#dod:cancer) attributed to all [risk factors](#dod:risk-factor). The remaining share represents deaths that would be expected to have occurred in the absence of these known risk factors.
caption:
Share of cancer deaths attributed to risk factors

The estimated share of deaths from cancers attributed to all risk factors. The remaining share represents deaths that would be expected to have occurred in the absence of these known risk factors.

This horizontal bar chart ranks cancer types by the percentage of global deaths in 2023 linked to known risk factors, with values ranging from 0.9% to 100%. Cervical cancer sits at the top with 100% of deaths attributed to risk factors, followed by mesothelioma at 91.7% and lung, tracheal and bronchus cancers at 76.4%. Larynx cancer registers 73.4%, while esophageal and colon and rectum cancers fall in the mid-50s — 56.4% and 56.2% respectively. Liver cancer stands at 48.2%, and lip and oral cavity cancers at 39.4%. Pancreatic, uterine, bladder, breast, and kidney cancers cluster between 27.9% and 38.7%. Nasopharynx, stomach, leukemia, thyroid, gallbladder and biliary tract, multiple myeloma, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, and prostate cancers occupy the lower half of the chart, with prostate cancer showing the smallest share at 0.9%. The methodology used to calculate these shares can include negative values in some cases, which may reflect potential protective effects or indicate uncertainty due to weak or poorly understood relationships between risk factors and health outcomes. The distribution shows a steep drop from cervical cancer down through the top five types, then a more gradual decline across the middle and lower tiers, with the bottom six cancers all falling below 20%.
